title: ClientRow component not memoized — 200+ rows re-render on every keystroke in search input
description: ClientRow (and CategorySection, ReservedSection, all Hero* cards) are declared as plain function components — none of them wrapped in React.memo. The parent ClientsPage holds `search`, `activeFilter`, `expandedId`, `selectedMonth`, `meetingForClient`, `claimForClient`, `toastMsg`, `newClientOpen`, `globalSearchOpen` as local state. A single keystroke in the search input (line 663) triggers re-render of ClientsPage which reconciles every CategorySection and each ClientRow inside (Grep confirmed `React.memo|memo(` returns zero matches in the file). With ~200-481 clients per manager (as per the comment at line 202 'У менеджера може бути 481+ клієнтів'), each character typed walks the full render tree including all ClientRow JSX subtrees with their date/icon/badge logic. Profiling on a 200-client base in this codebase will show >100ms scripting per keystroke.
impact: Search input lags on every keystroke for managers with 200+ clients; same cascade fires on filter pill clicks, expand toggle, and on every 60s meetings refresh / 30s notifications refresh / 15s claims comments refresh because those mutate context that ClientsPage subscribes to indirectly via shared SWR cache.
recommendation: Wrap ClientRow, CategorySection, ReservedSection in React.memo with custom comparator (compare client.ClientID + the slice of plan/fact/activity/focus this row needs). Debounce the search input (150ms) via useDeferredValue or a setTimeout so groupedClients useMemo doesn't recompute every keystroke. Alternative — virtualize the list (react-window/virtua) since lists of 200-481 don't need DOM presence for off-screen rows.

title: CategorySection recomputes 4 filter passes over the client array on every parent render without useMemo
description: Lines 1346-1361 compute `inProgressCount`, `completedCount`, `unplannedCount`, `emptyCount` with four separate `sorted.filter(...)` calls outside any useMemo. CategorySection receives `planByClient`, `factByClient`, `activityByClient`, `meetingMissingClientIds`, `focusByClient` as fresh object references on every parent render (ClientsPage useMemo dependencies include factByClient which itself reconstructs via mergeFactBreakdown), so this section reruns 4 O(n) scans per render across each of the up to 6 visible categories. Combined with finding #1 (no memo) this is ~24 full-array scans per keystroke.
impact: Multiplies the per-keystroke work in the search input and amplifies cascade re-renders triggered by 30s notification polling. Concrete cost: 6 categories × 4 filters × ~80 clients/category = ~1920 plan+fact dictionary lookups per keystroke.
recommendation: Compute the four counts in a single reduce inside one useMemo keyed on `[sorted, planByClient, factByClient]`, returning `{inProgress, completed, unplanned, empty}`. Or compute the counts in ClientsPage once across `groupedClients` and pass numbers down as props.

title: ClientsPage runs four parallel 1С Action chains (getSalesFact ×3, getClientFocus ×4, checkActivities ×3+) on every clients/list load
description: `useClientsTotals` (line 228-230) issues 3 useOneCData calls to getSalesFact (chunks of 400). `useClientFocuses` (line 276-279) issues 4 useOneCData calls to getClientFocus (chunks of 200). `useClientActivities` does the same pattern for checkActivities. Each useOneCData is a separate SWR key → separate fetch through /api/onec → separate 1С HTTP-сервіс call. On /clients mount a manager with 481 clients triggers: 1 getManagerClients + 2 getSalesFact chunks + 3 getClientFocus chunks + 3 checkActivities chunks + 1 getClientActivationPlan + 1 plan-totals + 1 registry plan = 11+ sequential network round-trips before Hero metrics resolve. Each 1С call has known cold-start latency (the auto-reload guard at line 315-331 exists specifically because 1С returns empty after timeout).
impact: First /clients load takes the slowest of those 11 round-trips end-to-end. Comment at line 207-210 confirms past observed bug: April plan showed $120K vs real $55K — a sign that out-of-order resolution and SWR cache stitching is fragile. Cold-start auto-reload after 30s (line 320) is a workaround for this exact fan-out, not a fix.
recommendation: Add one server-side aggregator endpoint `/api/clients/dashboard?login=X&month=YYYY-MM` that calls 1С in parallel server-side and returns `{clients, planByClient, factByClient, focusByClient, activityByClient, activationPlan}` in a single response. Cache on Vercel edge for 30s. This collapses 11 round-trips into 1 from the browser and removes the need for the auto-reload sessionStorage hack.

title: checkActivities/getClientFocus chunk sizing (3×200=600, 4×200=800) silently truncates managers with more clients
description: Comment at line 201-202 explicitly states 'У менеджера може бути 481+ клієнтів'. useClientFocuses caps at 4 chunks × 200 = 800. checkActivities caps at 3 × 200 = 600 (referenced in comment line 248). `chunkClientIds(ids, size, count)` (lib/client-batching.ts:43) returns exactly `count` chunks — extra clients past chunk[count]·size are silently dropped. If a manager onboards more clients, Hero Card 4 (Контактна активність) starts under-counting calls/meetings without any warning. Real 1С constraint is the per-request ID cap (200), not the chunk count — there's no reason to fix `count` at compile time.
impact: Silent data truncation. Manager with 700 clients sees Hero Card 4 'no contacts' inflated by 100 because checkActivities never asked about them. Already happened once for getSalesFact (3×400=1200 capacity for 481 clients — works today but breaks at 1200+).
recommendation: Replace fixed `[chunk1, chunk2, chunk3, chunk4]` destructuring with a dynamic SWR-array pattern (use a single SWR key built from sorted clientIds + month, and inside the fetcher Promise.all the dynamic number of chunks). Or add a runtime assert that throws/logs to Sentry when `clientIds.length > chunk_count × chunk_size`. Better — move to the server aggregator (finding #3) where chunking is internal.

title: Public screenshots served as un-optimized 600KB-1.1MB PNGs without next/image
description: ls -la shows: clients-01-hero.png = 1,107,597 bytes, clients-02-list.png = 783,014 bytes, clients-03-card.png = 653,190 bytes, meetings-01-list.png = 862,979 bytes, meetings-02-list-scroll.png = 861,285 bytes, 01-login.png = 529,377 bytes. All referenced from `public/manual.html` (raw tags lines 207, 270, 296, 310, 325...). manual.html is static HTML served directly from /public — next/image is not applicable to it, but neither are these PNGs converted to WebP/AVIF nor downscaled (the manual is viewed on laptops, not 4K screens). Total manual page weight on first open: ~7 MB of raster.
impact: Slow first paint of manual.html (the docs page managers open from the burger menu). On 3G mobile a manager waits 15-30s to see the user guide. Not user-facing for the planning workflow but cuts adoption of the manual.
recommendation: Run the screenshots through cwebp at quality 80 (typically yields 80-90% reduction for UI screenshots) and update manual.html to use ``. Or migrate manual.html to a Next.js page and use next/image with responsive sizes.

title: Three independent SWR pollers (30s/60s/15s) fire even when their UI is not visible
description: Three refreshInterval pollers run permanently while the user is on any authenticated page: notifications-bell.tsx:39 polls /api/notifications every 30_000ms, meetings/use-meetings.ts:128 polls /api/meetings every 60_000ms, claims/claim-detail-view.tsx:97 polls /api/claims/:id/comments every 15_000ms. None of them gate on `document.visibilityState === 'visible'` or pause when the dropdown/panel is closed. The notifications bell polls every 30s for every authenticated user even when the bell is collapsed and nothing has changed — that's 2880 calls/day per active user × ~25 users = 72k Vercel Function Invocations/day just for the bell. Meetings polls 60s while user is on any page (not just /meetings) because useMeetings is imported from places like clients-page (line 29).
impact: Vercel Function Invocation count inflated by ~100k/day for nothing — each poll hits Supabase + sometimes 1С. Mobile battery drain when app stays open in a background tab. SWR cache churn triggers cascade renders that compound finding #1.
recommendation: Wrap the polling with SWR's `isVisible` check or set `refreshWhenHidden: false` (it's the default in SWR 2.x but verify), and additionally tie meetings polling to scope === 'mine' && pathname.startsWith('/meetings'). For notifications-bell, only poll while bell open (drop interval to e.g. 10s when open, 5min when closed). For claim comments, only poll when the detail view is visible (already gated by route — OK). Add `revalidateIfStale: true; refreshWhenHidden: false; refreshWhenOffline: false` to all three.

title: Webhook secret comparison is timing-unsafe (plain !==)
description: `if (provided !== secret)` on line 56 uses ordinary string inequality. String comparison in V8 short-circuits at the first differing byte, leaking the matching prefix length via response timing. Although remote network jitter makes this hard to exploit in practice, it is the canonical anti-pattern for shared-secret auth and the SP_NOTIFICATIONS_SECRET protects an unauthenticated insert path that can write to any user's notifications.
impact: Defense-in-depth weakening on the only auth check for an externally callable webhook. Combined with infinite retry attempts (no rate limit, see other findings) the timing oracle becomes more realistic.
recommendation: Use `crypto.timingSafeEqual(Buffer.from(provided), Buffer.from(secret))` after a length check, or `node:crypto` `timingSafeEqual` on Uint8Arrays. Reject in constant time even when lengths differ.

title: Webhook accepts unbounded JSON bodies — no POST size limit
description: `await request.json()` on line 62 has no Content-Length guard. `body.meta` (line 90) is written straight into a `jsonb` column with no size validation, and `title` / `message` (lines 80-82, 88) have no length cap. Anyone with the shared secret (or a leaked one) can post multi-MB payloads, exhausting Vercel function memory and inflating Supabase storage.
impact: DoS amplification: a single secret-holder (or stolen Bitrix webhook secret) can blow up the notifications table and serverless function cost. The route uses the Supabase SERVICE ROLE key so writes succeed without any RLS check.
recommendation: Read `content-length` header first, reject > ~16 KB. Cap `title` to 200 chars, `message` to 2 KB, `meta` JSON string length to 4 KB before INSERT. Also bound `link` to a sane URL length.

title: Replay/deduplication is opt-in — webhook without dedupKey creates duplicates on retry
description: `dedup_key: body.dedupKey ?? null` (line 91) makes the dedup field optional. The UNIQUE index `notifications_dedup_uniq` in migration 20260610_021_notifications.sql lines 41-43 has `where dedup_key is not null`, so rows submitted without a dedupKey bypass it entirely. The header docblock claims idempotency for retries but only callers that remember to send dedupKey get it. There is also no nonce + timestamp validation, so an attacker replaying a captured request with dedupKey present is silently swallowed as `{success:true, deduplicated:true}` — no audit trail that a replay happened.
impact: Bitrix-style 5xx retry loops can spam the bell with duplicates for any integration that forgot dedupKey. Replay attacks (e.g. captured webhook) cannot be distinguished from legitimate retries.
recommendation: Require `dedupKey` (reject 400 when absent) OR auto-derive one server-side from `type + userLogin + meta` hash. Optionally add `timestamp` + ±5 min skew window and log replays for monitoring.

title: Secret rotation is unsafe — dual env vars accepted forever
description: Lines 47-48 accept EITHER `SP_NOTIFICATIONS_SECRET` OR the legacy `NOTIFICATIONS_INTERNAL_SECRET` via `??` fallback. If an operator rotates only the canonical name in Vercel, the legacy one (often still committed somewhere or shared with the Python webhook author) keeps working silently. There is no log when the legacy fallback is used, so leaked legacy values can be re-used indefinitely without anyone noticing.
impact: Rotation hygiene problem — leaked old secret cannot be effectively retired without deleting both env vars at once, and there is no telemetry to detect that the legacy path is still in use.
recommendation: Either pick one canonical name and hard-fail without it, or log a WARN every time the fallback resolves so you can see leftover callers in Vercel logs. Add a runbook to delete the legacy var after migration.

title: Manual sync route resets retry_count on every admin click — bypasses MAX_RETRIES guard
description: Line 104: `.update({ status: 'syncing', retry_count: 0 })` claims pending/failed rows and zeroes retry_count. Failed rows that already hit `MAX_RETRIES = 2` (line 28) get their counter reset to 0 every time an admin opens this endpoint, then are POSTed to 1C again. If the 1C error is permanent (bad payload, deleted client, etc.), each admin click resends the broken row up to 2 more times. There is no per-row cooldown and no rate limit on the endpoint itself.
impact: A frustrated admin (or any cross-site state where Sec-Fetch-Site reports same-origin from a stored-XSS payload) can issue unbounded retries to 1С for permanently broken rows, hammering the 1С HTTP service. The status remains `failed` only as long as nobody opens the DLQ page.
recommendation: Only reset retry_count when admin explicitly invokes /api/admin/sync-dlq with op=retry on a specific id (sync-dlq line 60 already does this). In sync-meetings-now, claim only `status='pending'` (do not include failed) and preserve retry_count.

title: Internal webhook does not verify that userLogin maps to a real user
description: Validation on lines 67-73 only checks that `userLogin` is non-empty and contains `@`. There is no lookup against the users table, so any string like `attacker@evil.com` or a typo like `sm.dnper2@emet.in.ua` (note the typo) is accepted and stored. Notifications addressed to non-existent logins silently accumulate and are never displayed or cleaned up. Combined with the missing size limit, this is also a low-cost way to grow the table indefinitely.
impact: Silent data-loss class bug: typos in upstream integrations are not surfaced. Over time the notifications table accumulates orphan rows and the unread-count query (notifications/route.ts line 48) is wasted on dead rows.
recommendation: Validate `userLogin` against the users table (or at minimum against an allow-list of `@emet.in.ua` / known domains) before INSERT. Return 404 for unknown logins so upstream callers see typos immediately.

title: No root Error Boundary in production — uncaught render error = white screen
description: Confirmed: src/app/error.tsx was reverted today (caa2b25) and there is no src/app/global-error.tsx either (Glob returned empty). RootLayout has no React Error Boundary wrapper; only TooltipProvider/SessionBootstrap. The Next 15 App Router falls back to the framework default — a blank page or `_error` html with no EMET branding, no 'reload' CTA, and no Sentry capture trigger (Sentry captures the throw but the user sees nothing actionable).
impact: Any uncaught client render error (the kind that produced SALES-PLANNING-2) now drops the entire UI to a blank screen for the manager mid-planning. No 'try again' button. Manager loses unsaved input in planning-form, can only F5.
recommendation: Re-add src/app/global-error.tsx (must include <html><body>) AND src/app/error.tsx, both calling Sentry.captureException(error) and showing a styled 'щось пішло не так — спробуйте оновити' page with a Reset button (router.refresh + reset()). Cover at minimum: dashboard (/), planning (/planning/[period]), admin/* segments.

title: 1С proxy logs full request payload (including new-client PII) to Vercel logs
description: `console.log('[ШАГ 1] Відправка в 1С для дії...', JSON.stringify({ action, payload: safePayload }, null, 2))` always runs (no env guard). For action='registerNewClient' the safePayload contains {managerLogin, name, phone, address, education, files:[{name,type,contentBase64}]} — a real client phone and home/clinic address are dumped to Vercel function logs. `saveClientSurvey` dumps the full client survey JSON (sensitive client data) the same way. Files contentBase64 is also serialized — multi-MB document base64 payloads end up in logs, breaking the 4KB log-line limit and likely truncating other context.
impact: PII (phone, address, education) lands in Vercel logs accessible to anyone with project Owner/Member role. GDPR exposure. Also defeats the audit you did earlier (B2 preview scrubbing). Base64 attachments make individual log lines huge and pollute log retention.
recommendation: Build a redactor: if action in ('registerNewClient','saveClientSurvey','findClient') log only `{action, fields:Object.keys(safePayload), fileCount: safePayload.files?.length}`. Strip `contentBase64`, mask `phone` to last 4 digits, drop `address`/`education`/`surveyData`. Mirror the same redactor in src/app/api/cron/sync-meetings/route.ts:205 and src/app/api/admin/sync-meetings-now/route.ts:120 which log call.payload identically.

title: Debug log left in production claims/[id] route — dumps Bitrix file metadata
description: `console.log('[claims/${claimId}] ufCrm4_FILES raw:', JSON.stringify(raw).slice(0, 800))` runs on every GET of a claim detail. The comment literally says 'Видалити після фіксу' but it's still there. Logged on every claim view (SWR refreshes on focus → every tab switch).
impact: Bitrix file metadata (urls, names, ids) written to Vercel logs on every claim open. Inflates log volume, plus reveals internal Bitrix file IDs that should not be in long-retention logs. Was intended as temporary diagnostic.
recommendation: Remove the console.log entirely, or gate behind `if (process.env.NODE_ENV !== 'production')`. Same audit needed for `[claims/${claimId}/file] Bitrix JSON keys:` at src/app/api/claims/[id]/file/route.ts:181 and `[claim created]` at src/app/claims/new/page.tsx:72.

title: Sentry source maps NOT uploaded — production stack traces are unreadable
description: withSentryConfig is called without `authToken` or `org`/`project` and no SENTRY_AUTH_TOKEN env is referenced. The inline comment confirms it: 'Source maps upload не активуємо поки нема SENTRY_AUTH_TOKEN'. Result: every Sentry issue (SALES-PLANNING-2 included) shows minified frames like `t.a (page-abc123.js:1:4521)` and you cannot tell which component or file threw.
impact: When the next white-screen incident hits production, you can see in Sentry that an error happened but cannot route-cause it. Significantly slows debugging the very class of bug (SALES-PLANNING-2) you tried to fix today.
recommendation: Generate a Sentry auth token (Settings → Auth Tokens → 'project:releases' + 'project:write'), set SENTRY_AUTH_TOKEN in Vercel env (Production only), pass `{ org, project, authToken: process.env.SENTRY_AUTH_TOKEN, widenClientFileUpload: true, hideSourceMaps: true }` to withSentryConfig. Source maps will upload and be auto-deleted from the browser bundle.

title: Sentry init has no `beforeSend` PII scrubber
description: Both Sentry.init calls (sentry.client.config.ts:17 and src/instrumentation.ts:19) lack `beforeSend` and `beforeBreadcrumb` hooks. When `setError((e as Error).message)` happens in new-client-dialog.tsx:171 after a failed `registerNewClient`, the OneCError message can contain the upstream 1С error which sometimes echoes the phone/name from the payload. Sentry browser SDK auto-captures URLs, request bodies via the BrowserTracing integration, and fetch/XHR breadcrumbs that include URLs like `/api/claims/{id}/comments` with form-data payloads. No filtering = client phone numbers, clinic names, and session.login values can be sent to sentry.io.
impact: Personal data of clients (phones, names) and managers (login emails) is uploaded to a third-party service without consent filtering. GDPR data-processor concern. If Sentry account is breached, EMET client base leaks.
recommendation: Add `beforeSend(event){ if(event.request?.data) event.request.data = '[scrubbed]'; if(event.user?.email) event.user.email = event.user.email.split('@')[1] ? `*@${event.user.email.split('@')[1]}` : '***'; return event; }` and `beforeBreadcrumb(b){ if(b.category==='fetch' && b.data?.url?.includes('/api/onec')) b.data.body=undefined; return b; }` in BOTH configs. Also strip `Authorization` headers.

title: Fire-and-forget fetch chains only console.warn — invisible failures in prod
description: Two background syncs swallow errors: `fetch('/api/planning/confirm-activities', ...).catch(err => console.warn('[confirm-activities] failed', err))` at line 750 and the same pattern for `/api/planning/init-snapshot` at line 902. console.warn from the browser does NOT reach Vercel logs or Sentry — it dies in the user's DevTools console which they never open. Result: if confirm-activities POST starts failing in production (e.g. CSRF token rotation, DB outage), planned activities silently stop persisting and you have zero signal.
impact: Silent data loss for activity confirmations. Manager checks a 'Дзвінок' checkbox, sees it persisted in UI state, but the row never lands in Supabase. Discovered weeks later when reports diverge.
recommendation: Replace `.catch(err => console.warn(...))` with `.catch(err => { console.warn(...); Sentry.captureException(err, { tags: { area: 'confirm-activities' } }); })`. Same fix at planning-form.tsx:902 for init-snapshot.

title: Login route logs the manager's login on every 1С failure
description: `console.error('[login] 1С fetch failed:', e instanceof Error ? e.message : String(e))` does NOT log `login` directly, BUT the rate-limit key on line 37 builds `login:${ip}` and the upstream request body sent on line 93 contains the password. While this specific console.error excludes credentials, /api/auth/login does NOT include any structured log tagging the attempted login — which means when 1С is down you cannot tell from Vercel logs WHICH manager is being locked out by 429 (rate limit returns at line 39 with no log). Operationally this slows recovery.
impact: When 1С goes down or rate-limit fires, support cannot quickly see which logins are affected. Just a log/observability gap, not a leak.
recommendation: Add `console.warn('[login] rate-limited', { loginHash: crypto.createHash('sha256').update(login).digest('hex').slice(0,8), ip })` before returning the 429 at line 39, and `console.warn('[login] failed', { loginHash, code: 'onec_unavailable' })` after the fetch fails. Use hash, not raw login, to balance debuggability vs PII.

title: `[ШАГ 2]` logs upstream 1С response body (up to 1KB) — leaks client data into Vercel logs
description: Line 257: `console.log('[ШАГ 2] Відповідь від 1С "${action}" OK', truncated)` where `truncated` is `JSON.stringify(json).slice(0,1000)`. For actions like `getClientReport`, `getManagerClients`, `getClientFocus`, `findClient`, `getAllMeetingsForClient`, the first 1000 chars contain client names, ClientIDs, phone numbers and addresses. Every successful 1С call writes this to Vercel logs. The log claim handlers similarly log `Bitrix JSON keys` slices.
impact: Continuous PII spill into Vercel log retention (default 30 days on Pro). Anyone with project access browses client phone/name history. Violates data-minimization principle. Multiplied by ~5-10 1С calls per manager session.
recommendation: Define an `action → log-mode` map: for read-bulk actions (`getClientReport`, `getManagerClients`, `findClient`, `getClientFocus`, `getAllMeetingsForClient`, `getInitialData`) log only `{action, status, durationMs, itemCount: data?.clients?.length ?? data?.length}`. Reserve full-body logging for `getRegionData`/admin-debug actions where it's structural metrics, not PII. Or gate the body log behind `process.env.ONEC_VERBOSE_LOG === '1'` so prod is silent by default.

title: IDOR: getClientReport / getAllMeetingsForClient — будь-який авторизований менеджер бачить звіт чужого клієнта
description: У whitelist ALLOWED_ACTIONS дозволені 1С-actions `getClientReport` та `getAllMeetingsForClient`, які приймають лише `clientID` без `login`/`managerLogin`. Код явно констатує у коментарі (рядки 86-90): "1С не валідовує що цей clientID належить call manager... Якщо хтось наскрипчить — побачить чужий звіт." Менеджер з валідною cookie може зробити POST /api/onec {action:'getClientReport', payload:{clientID:'X'}} для будь-якого clientID (наприклад, перебором по getManagerClients інших регіонів через прямий запит) і отримати 3-міс історію продажів, події, контакти, ПІБ юзера. Same для getAllMeetingsForClient — список зустрічей з адресами/коментарями.
impact: Витік клієнтських даних (історія, активність, контакти) між менеджерами компанії. Для приватних практик/аптек це чутлива комерційна інформація — конкуренти всередині мережі можуть бачити чужу клієнтську базу.
recommendation: Додати ці actions у CLIENT_OWNERSHIP_BOUND список: на сервері тягнути getManagerClients(session.login) і перевіряти `clientID ∈ ownClients`, інакше 403. Як швидкий фікс — попросити 1С-розробника додати `login` у payload, валідувати на стороні 1С через регістр КлієнтиМенеджерів. Memory `meeting_app_actions_available` підтверджує що 1С знає прив'язку — треба її задіяти.

title: /api/admin/sync-dlq op=skip дозволено director-у — тиха втрата meeting-sync без подальшого запису у 1С
description: POST /api/admin/sync-dlq з body `{op:'skip', id}` маркує meeting_syncs row як `synced` БЕЗ виклику 1С (`failure_reason: 'skipped manually by ${session.login}'`). Guard — `session.role === 'admin' || 'director'`. Memory `director_credentials` (sdu@) має директорський доступ і може за помилку клікнути «Skip» у UI, що тихо втратить save/start/finish meeting без сліду у 1С. Менеджер потім бачить статус 'synced' у себе і думає, що зустріч у CRM, хоча її там нема.
impact: Дані втрачаються мовчки: 1С не знає про зустріч (нема ні saveNewMeeting ні start/finish), а в БД sales-planning і у меню менеджера зустріч позначена як «синхронізована». Касовий звіт у 1С і дашборд у sales-planning розходяться без аудит-сліду чому. Director сам не побачить цей лосс — повідомлень менеджерам нема.
recommendation: Обмежити `op=skip` лише `session.role==='admin'` (один акаунт itd@), або додати confirm-step з обов'язковим `reason` у body що пишеться у audit-table + Telegram-нотіф адміну. Director залишити лише `op=retry`. Це симетрично до /admin/planning-locks де director теж не може unfinalize.

title: Admin/Director не можуть редагувати/завершити зустріч керованого менеджера — start/finish від супровідного юзера падає 404
description: PATCH /api/meetings/[id] викликає `updateMeeting(session.login, id, ...)` / `startMeeting(session.login, id, ...)` / `finishMeeting(session.login, id, ...)`. У repo.ts (рядки 290-298 та 350-356) SELECT робиться з `.eq('manager_login', session.login)` — повертає row тільки якщо session.login == manager_login. Тому admin/director може ЧИТАТИ чужі зустрічі через GET ?scope=managed, але PATCH повертає 'meeting not found or not owned' (404) — функціональний gap. Це не security vuln, але `/api/meetings` GET вже декларує що privileged ролі мають оверсайт, а PATCH-ендпоінт цю модель порушує.
impact: Admin не може допомогти менеджеру виправити зависле in_progress meeting (наприклад, скасувати чи finish від його імені) без прямого SQL у Supabase. У продакшені після крашу телефону менеджера на полі це означає що зустріч навічно `in_progress`.
recommendation: Розширити signature `updateMeeting/startMeeting/finishMeeting` другим параметром `actor` (для запису у audit) та змінити ownership-check: дозволити patch якщо `session.role==='admin'` АБО `session.managedUsers.includes(meeting.manager_login)`. Лог у БД хто реально натискав (`updated_by` колонка).

title: /api/cron/sync-meetings — небезпечне порівняння CRON_SECRET через простий `!==`
description: Auth перевірка `if (auth !== \`Bearer ${expected}\`) → 401`. Не timing-safe. Теоретично атакувальник може через timing-attack відновити CRON_SECRET. На Vercel практичний impact низький (мережева латентність маскує мікросекунди), але best practice — `crypto.timingSafeEqual(Buffer.from(provided), Buffer.from(expected))`. Те саме у `/api/notifications/internal` (рядок 56) для SP_NOTIFICATIONS_SECRET.
impact: Якщо хтось скомпрометує endpoint timing — отримає CRON_SECRET. Тоді може довільно тригерити sync-meetings, dump-ити Logs з Vercel, або в /api/notifications/internal створювати фейкові сповіщення менеджерам.
recommendation: Замінити обидва порівняння на `crypto.timingSafeEqual` з paddingом до однакової довжини. Helper: `function safeEq(a,b){try{const A=Buffer.from(a);const B=Buffer.from(b);if(A.length!==B.length)return false;return crypto.timingSafeEqual(A,B);}catch{return false;}}`.

title: Director може писати planning/finalize/init-snapshot керованих менеджерів попри коментар «director read-only»
description: Коментарі у /api/planning POST (рядок 161), /api/planning/finalize (рядок 21), /api/planning/init-snapshot (рядок 86) кажуть: «Director: ТІЛЬКИ власний логін (read-only роль; чужі плани не пише)». Але код фактично перевіряє: `effectiveLogin !== session.login && session.role !== 'admin' && !isMultiRegionRM && !session.managedUsers.includes(effectiveLogin)`. Role==='director' НЕ виключається — якщо у director-а є managedUsers (sdu@ має 4 за memory `director_credentials`), він пройде guard через `managedUsers.includes(effectiveLogin)`. Тобто sdu@ може фіналізувати/розфіналізовувати/писати snapshot для 4 своїх менеджерів — що суперечить документації коду.
impact: Якщо product owner вважає що director read-only (за коментарями + memory), то ефективна модель — "director може писати за 4 менеджерів" — це непомічений drift. Менеджер може поскаржитись «хтось змінив мій план» і важко буде довести/audit. У M10 `can_unfinalize_plans` дозволяється toggle, але fallback для director лишається — він може просто PATCH-нути finalized_at через POST.
recommendation: Або (а) видалити коментарі про read-only і явно задокументувати що director має write для managedUsers, або (б) додати окрему перевірку `if (session.role==='director' && effectiveLogin !== session.login) return 403`. Memory підказує що дизайн був (б) — треба підтвердити з користувачем і додати тест.

title: /api/planning POST: window-guard обходиться при isFinalized+allowStageEdit для будь-якого target — без чек admin/permission по сесії
description: Логіка: `if (!(isFinalized && allowStageEdit)) { winCheck = assertWindowAllowed(...); }`. Тобто якщо план фіналізований І у TARGET-юзера (`uid`) виставлено `can_edit_stages_after_finalize=true`, window-lock пропускається. Але `uid` = effectiveLogin (drill-down target), не session.login. Multi-region RM (Пашковська) проходить scope-check (через MULTI_REGION_RM_OVERRIDES) і може писати в plan керованого менеджера у іншому регіоні де у цього менеджера M9-permission увімкнено. Window-lock компанії на цей місяць (наприклад, lock через грудень 2025 для фінансової фіксації) не діє. Це не explicit IDOR, але приховує bypass через target-користувача.
impact: Якщо admin виставив M9-permission для одного менеджера для legitimate use case (правка етапу), Multi-region RM міг увійти і змінити stage_comment у будь-який день, обходячи window-lock який мав би діяти на всіх. У звітах це виглядатиме як легітимне редагування фіналізованого плану від РМ-а — без сліду що це поза вікном.
recommendation: Зчитувати `allowStageEdit` з `session.login` а не з `uid` (тоді admin/permission прив'язана до actor, не до target). Або явно дозволити обхід тільки коли `session.login === uid` (менеджер сам собі править етап). Window-lock — політика компанії, а не персональний дозвіл цільового користувача.

title: /api/notifications/internal приймає довільний userLogin без верифікації існування — spam-вектор при витоку SP_NOTIFICATIONS_SECRET
description: Endpoint валідує лише формат email (`userLogin.includes('@')`) і існування type/title. Не перевіряє, чи userLogin реально існує у `users`. Якщо SP_NOTIFICATIONS_SECRET витече (memory `reclamation_app_secrets` каже що app-password у git і ще не rotated), атакувальник може спамити сповіщення на довільні email-адреси (включно з зовнішніми), використовуючи нашу базу як notify-relay. Sender bitrix:claim може мати фейкові link → відкриває phishing redirect для менеджерів.
impact: Notification Center менеджера засмічений підробленими повідомленнями з посиланнями на зовнішні домени; підрив довіри до системи; потенційне phishing-вектор всередині компанії.
recommendation: (а) Rotate SP_NOTIFICATIONS_SECRET (memory вже відмічене як TODO для Sprint D); (б) Додати white-list домену для userLogin (тільки `@emet.in.ua`/`@emet.com`); (в) Whitelist host у `body.link` (тільки внутрішні URL — `/claims/*`, `/meetings/*`); (г) Rate-limit per `dedupKey` prefix для запобігання spam-у.

title: /api/onec registerNewClient: admin/director може створити клієнта у 1С під логіном будь-якого менеджера
description: MANAGER_LOGIN_BOUND_ACTIONS перевіряє: `if (!requested) → override до session.login; else if requested !== session.login && !managedUsers.includes && !isAdminOrDirector → 403`. Тобто admin/director обходять scope і можуть викликати `registerNewClient({managerLogin: 'будь-хто', name, phone, address...})`. 1С запише нового клієнта у регістр під довільним менеджером. Для admin (itd@) це debug-tool ОК (зазначено у коментарі), але для director (sdu@) це може створити невірний owner у CRM, який потім стане частиною kpi/факту менеджера.
impact: Director можу присвоїти клієнта будь-якому менеджеру (свідомо або помилково). Це впливає на attribution продажів, KPI, бонуси менеджерів. Якщо clientID потім піде у Action 5 — факт цього клієнта попадає в звіт іншого менеджера.
recommendation: Для director (і Multi-region RM) обмежити `registerNewClient` сторонньо: дозволити лише якщо `requested ∈ session.managedUsers ∪ {session.login}`. Admin лишається без обмежень. Або вивести цей action у окремий admin-only endpoint /api/admin/clients/register і ховати з UI у не-admin.

title: getRegistryPlans exposes all managers' plans to any logged-in user
description: `getRegistryPlans` is in ALLOWED_ACTIONS but absent from both LOGIN_BOUND_ACTIONS and MANAGER_LOGIN_BOUND_ACTIONS. Request shape is only {dateFrom, dateTo} (onec-types.ts:121-125) — there is no per-manager parameter to override. Response carries every manager's planAmountUSD plus divisionCode, managerLogin, managerName for every segment across the whole company (onec-types.ts:127-143). use-registry-plans.ts filters client-side, but the API itself happily returns the full set. Any authenticated manager can POST `{action:'getRegistryPlans', payload:{dateFrom,dateTo}}` and harvest the entire company's USD plan registry, including peers in other regions.
impact: Cross-manager / cross-region plan disclosure: monthly USD targets per segment for every manager in 1С are reachable by any session. This is the same class of data the includeAll guard on getRegionData explicitly tries to protect, but it is reachable here through a different action.
recommendation: Either (a) make /api/onec call getRegistryPlans on behalf of session.login and filter response.plans on the server before returning (drop rows where row.managerLogin is not the caller, not in session.managedUsers, not admin/director/multi-region RM), or (b) restrict the raw action to admin/director only and provide a thin endpoint for managers that returns only their own plans.

title: getClientReport / getAllMeetingsForClient / saveClientSurvey accept any clientID without ownership check
description: The TODO at lines 86-90 acknowledges that 1С does not validate that the supplied clientID belongs to the caller, but the proxy still forwards `{clientID}` payloads as-is for getClientReport, getAllMeetingsForClient and (worse, write-side) saveClientSurvey. `saveClientSurvey({clientID, surveyData})` (onec-types.ts:412-416) has no login or managerLogin field, so a hostile or curious manager can not only READ another manager's 3-month sales history, meetings, events and clientInfo, but also OVERWRITE the survey data of any client in 1С by guessing or harvesting a clientID. findClient returns ClientCategory and Phone for any client across the company, which is a reasonable seed source for valid clientIDs.
impact: Cross-manager client-data leak (history, events, phone, education) and arbitrary write to another manager's client survey. "Risk acceptable because UI only renders own IDs" relies on browser self-discipline; bypass is `fetch('/api/onec',{...})` from devtools.
recommendation: Build a server-side ownership index per session (cache result of getManagerClients keyed by session.login, TTL ~5 min). For getClientReport / getAllMeetingsForClient / saveClientSurvey, reject payloads whose clientID is not in that set, unless session.role is admin/director or the clientID belongs to a managedUsers report. Until 1С adds login-binding, this is the only enforcement layer.

title: getTrainings is unscoped — any manager can read other regions' training plans
description: getTrainings is allowed but appears in neither LOGIN_BOUND_ACTIONS nor MANAGER_LOGIN_BOUND_ACTIONS. Its payload is `{regionCode, dateFrom}` (onec-types.ts:236-241), so any authenticated user can request training data for an arbitrary regionCode (KYV, DNP, ODS, …). Worth noting that getTrainings IS in ADMIN_PROXY_ACTIONS (line 38), but that branch is reached only inside the `if (LOGIN_BOUND_ACTIONS.has(action))` block at line 154 — since getTrainings is not in LOGIN_BOUND_ACTIONS the ADMIN_PROXY_ACTIONS handling for it is dead code, masking the missing scope check.
impact: Cross-region disclosure of training schedules (trainingName/type/date/city per region). Lower business impact than plans, but it's still data the proxy was clearly intended to scope (the admin-proxy comment treats it as 'company-wide').
recommendation: Override safePayload.regionCode from session.regionCode for managers/RMs; allow admin/director (and admin via DIRECTOR_PROXY_LOGIN if needed) to request other regions. Either move the admin-proxy block out of the LOGIN_BOUND_ACTIONS guard, or add a separate REGION_BOUND_ACTIONS set.

title: Request payload is console.log'd in full, including PII and base64 attachments
description: `console.log('[ШАГ 1] Відправка в 1С ...', JSON.stringify({action, payload: safePayload}, null, 2))` writes the entire outgoing payload to Vercel logs. For registerNewClient that includes client phone, address, education, plus every file as base64 (`RegisterNewClientFile.contentBase64`, onec-types.ts:374-379) — passports, documents, photos. For saveClientSurvey it includes whatever surveyData JSON the meeting outcome form produced. Vercel runtime logs are retained and visible to any team member with project access.
impact: Client PII and uploaded documents are written into log storage in cleartext (base64 is not encryption). Log retention turns an ephemeral 1С call into a long-lived copy of personal data; large base64 also blows up log volume / cost.
recommendation: Replace the raw payload dump with a redacted version: log only action, top-level keys, and counts (e.g. `files: 3 items, 1.2 MB`). Strip phone/address/files entirely; if debugging is needed, gate the full dump behind `process.env.ONEC_DEBUG === '1'` and never enable it on prod.

title: Upstream error message echoed to client can leak 1С infrastructure details
description: On fetch failure the catch block returns `"Помилка зв'язку з 1С: " + err.message` straight to the browser. Node's fetch / undici puts host, IP, port and reason inside that message (e.g. `connect ECONNREFUSED 10.42.1.7:8080`, `getaddrinfo ENOTFOUND 1c-internal.emet.local`, full TLS error chains). The non-JSON branch at line 241-249 was carefully sanitised in prod, but this generic-catch branch was not.
impact: Information disclosure of internal 1С hostname / private IP / port / auth status to any authenticated user (and to anyone who can trigger a 502 by spamming during 1С maintenance). Useful reconnaissance for an attacker who has stolen a session.
recommendation: Match the JSON-parse branch: in production return a fixed `'1С тимчасово недоступна'` message and put `err.message` only behind `process.env.NODE_ENV !== 'production'` (debugMessage field). Keep the full message in server logs.

title: includeAll guard runs only inside the LOGIN_BOUND_ACTIONS branch
description: The `if ('includeAll' in safePayload && session.role !== 'admin')` strip is nested inside `if (LOGIN_BOUND_ACTIONS.has(action))` (line 154). Today the only action that respects includeAll is getRegionData, which IS login-bound, so the guard fires. But the placement makes it easy to regress: any new ALLOWED_ACTION that is not login-bound (the next 'admin-only flag' use case) will silently bypass the strip, because the surrounding `if` short-circuits the entire block.
impact: Latent footgun. Today benign; future-self / next refactor likely to introduce a new admin-only flag (e.g. `includeArchived`, `allDivisions`) and put the check next to includeAll — and lose enforcement for any action that is not login-bound.
recommendation: Pull the `includeAll` (and any future admin-only flag) strip into a top-level pass that runs for every action regardless of login binding. Same for `includeAll` derivatives — make a small `ADMIN_ONLY_PAYLOAD_FLAGS` set and iterate.

title: MANAGER_LOGIN_BOUND_ACTIONS does not block multi-region RM from impersonating outside scope
description: The LOGIN_BOUND_ACTIONS branch (line 154-185) treats `isMultiRegionRM` as equivalent to admin/director — it can pass any `login` because Пашковська legitimately needs cross-region drill-down. But the MANAGER_LOGIN_BOUND_ACTIONS branch (line 191-206) checks only `isAdminOrDirector` and does NOT recognise `isMultiRegionRM`. As a result, a multi-region RM gets blocked at line 201-204 when she tries to call findClient or registerNewClient with another region's managerLogin — even though the very point of multi-region overrides is to let her work across both regions. Inverse asymmetry to the read path.
impact: Functional inconsistency more than security: Пашковська can drill into the other region for read-only actions but cannot search a client by managerLogin or register a client on behalf of a Mykolaiv manager. Likely silently degrades a corner of the CRM UI for her.
recommendation: Mirror the LOGIN_BOUND_ACTIONS logic: add `|| isMultiRegionRM` to the forbidden-condition at line 199-200, computing `isMultiRegionRM` from `MULTI_REGION_RM_OVERRIDES[session.login.toLowerCase().trim()]` at the top of the block. Add a short comment explaining the cross-region exception.

title: Per-session rate limit also acts as a per-user DoS vector across devices
description: Rate-limit key is `onec:${session.login}` (line 105). On a single Vercel instance two devices logged in as the same user share one bucket of 60 req/min — opening the app on phone + desktop with auto-refresh, hard-reload of /clients (which fan-outs to up to 4×4 chunked checkActivities + 4 chunked getClientFocus + 3 getSalesFact = ~11 calls per page load) plus background SWR revalidate-on-focus easily approaches the limit. Across Vercel instances the limit is multiplied by N instances (acknowledged in rate-limit.ts), so attacker-side enforcement is weak while legitimate-user-side enforcement is tight. No race per se (single-threaded event loop within an instance), but the policy itself is the wrong shape.
impact: Two devices for one user can stack and produce spurious 429s under normal use (especially during the auto-retry storm in useOneCData when 1С cold-starts and fires up to 25 polls). Conversely, a real DDoS routed across instances sees a much weaker ceiling. Net: bad UX for honest users, weak protection against the threat it nominally addresses.
recommendation: Split: keep `onec:${session.login}` at a more generous ceiling (say 200/min) for UX, and add a separate IP-based or `onec-write:${session.login}` bucket at lower limits for write-style actions (saveClientSurvey, registerNewClient, saveNewMeeting if it ever moves to this proxy). For real DDoS-grade protection move to Upstash Redis as the file's own TODO notes. Independently, deduplicate the SWR-driven auto-retry: when isEmptyResponse keeps firing, also short-circuit duplicate parallel chunk calls.

title: Real production secrets stored in local .env with no rotation guarantee
description: `.env` (gitignored, confirmed via `git check-ignore`) contains live production credentials: Supabase SERVICE_ROLE_KEY (signed JWT for ref vncfowqwzrefauqvaolx, valid until 2036), Postgres password `SalesPlanning2026!` (lines 7, 10), and `BITRIX_WEBHOOK_URL` token `g23ozkh3oho02o3e` (line 50). Service role bypasses all Supabase RLS. Anyone with read access to the laptop or any backup/dump containing this file gets full DB + Bitrix write. None of these were found in git history (good), but a single accidental `git add -A` or AI-tool with broader read scope leaks everything. Memory note also says this same Bitrix token previously was hardcoded in `reclamation-app` git history needing rotation — it still has not been rotated.
impact: Full Supabase read/write (bypasses RLS), full Bitrix24 CRM write, plus director user takeover via BACKFILL_PASSWORD. If any of these leak, attacker gets the entire CRM and customer reclamation data.
recommendation: 1) Rotate Bitrix24 webhook token (the one with `g23ozkh3oho02o3e` was reportedly committed in reclamation-app — assume compromised). 2) Rotate Supabase service role key + Postgres password. 3) Move dev-only QA/BACKFILL credentials out of `.env` into a separate `.env.qa.local` file or a password manager. 4) Add `git secret-scan` pre-commit hook (gitleaks).

title: Plain-text director and manager passwords in .env (BACKFILL_PASSWORD, QA_PASSWORD)
description: Real production user credentials sit in plain text in `.env`: `BACKFILL_LOGIN=sdu@emet.in.ua` + `BACKFILL_PASSWORD=CrmEmet0507D` (line 38-39) — this is the director (Саша) who can edit plans of all 21 managers and view all company data. Also `QA_LOGIN=sm.kiev4@emet.in.ua` + `QA_PASSWORD=CrmEmet83a` (line 29-30) — a real manager. These are used by `scripts/resnapshot-clients.mjs`, `scripts/qa-action7.mjs`, `scripts/diag-*.mjs` for one-off runs. Storing real prod passwords in a developer dotfile means they get included in every backup of the dev machine and travel through dropbox/icloud sync.
impact: Director account takeover if the dev machine, a backup of it, or a tar of project files leaks. Director can mutate any manager's plan and view all company data.
recommendation: Move these to a separate `.env.scripts.local` (ignored, not loaded by Next.js) and require explicit `cross-env BACKFILL_PASSWORD=... npm run snapshot:backfill` for one-off runs. Or use 1С service-account login for backfill scripts instead of a human director credential.

title: NEXT_PUBLIC_DEMO_LOGIN=true in .env enables password-less director login if shipped to prod
description: Local `.env` sets `NEXT_PUBLIC_DEMO_LOGIN=true` (line 23). When this flag is `true`, `/api/auth/login` accepts `{demo:true, login:'sdu@emet.in.ua'}` with NO password and returns a signed session cookie for the director (or any MOCK_USERS entry). The `.env.example` correctly warns about this and defaults to `false`, but if the developer ever copies their working `.env` to Vercel env or if Vercel env is missing and falls back, prod becomes wide-open. Memory note `demo_vs_real_logins.md` already flags that real users live under `@emet.in.ua` (which IS one of the MOCK_USERS keys).
impact: Anyone who can reach the prod URL can POST `{demo:true, login:'sdu@emet.in.ua'}` and get a director session cookie. No password, no 1С check.
recommendation: 1) Verify Vercel prod env has `NEXT_PUBLIC_DEMO_LOGIN=false` (or unset) — this is critical before any deploy. 2) Harden the route: even when flag is on, only allow demo logins whose login does NOT match a real `@emet.in.ua` user (i.e. only allow synthetic test accounts like `feshchenko@emet.com`). 3) Add a runtime warning banner in the UI when demo is on so devs notice if prod accidentally has it.

title: API_SECRET_KEY falls back to `dev-secret-key-change-me` in non-production environments
description: `getApiSecret()` returns the literal string `'dev-secret-key-change-me'` whenever `NODE_ENV !== 'production'` AND `API_SECRET_KEY` is unset. Vercel Preview deploys (PR previews, branch deploys) run with `NODE_ENV=production` so they are covered, but any self-hosted/staging/test deploy where NODE_ENV is left as `development` (common on internal Docker boxes) gets a publicly-known default key. Combined with line 73 `if (process.env.NODE_ENV !== 'production') return { valid: true }` in `validateApiRequest`, any non-prod deployment fully bypasses API auth — risk only if such an environment is ever exposed to the network.
impact: Non-prod deployments accept any/no key, allowing external callers to hit internal API endpoints. Not exploitable from outside prod today, but a foot-gun for any future staging deploy.
recommendation: Require `API_SECRET_KEY` even in dev (throw on startup if missing) — or generate a random per-process key when missing instead of a known string. Document that any deployed (non-localhost) environment must set `NODE_ENV=production`.

title: .env.example contains a real Bitrix webhook URL hint that mirrors live structure
description: `.env.example` line 48 has `BITRIX_WEBHOOK_URL="https://bitrix.emet.in.ua/rest/USER_ID/WEBHOOK_TOKEN/"`. The host `bitrix.emet.in.ua` is the real production Bitrix24 host. While the token is masked (`WEBHOOK_TOKEN`), the comment block correctly flags the security risk. This is informational leakage only (the host is already discoverable), but worth noting: keep `.env.example` placeholder hosts generic (`https://your-bitrix.example.com/...`) to avoid accidentally signalling infra topology in public repos.
impact: Low — host is already publicly accessible. Just reveals one piece of infra info to anyone reading the public GitHub repo.
recommendation: Replace `bitrix.emet.in.ua` in `.env.example` with `https://your-bitrix-instance.example.com/...`. Same for the Supabase URL pattern.

title: Sentry DSN exposed as NEXT_PUBLIC_SENTRY_DSN — verify it isn't a write-token
description: `instrumentation.ts:13` and `sentry.client.config.ts:14` read `NEXT_PUBLIC_SENTRY_DSN`. Sentry DSNs are designed to be public (write-only event ingestion key, no read), so this is normally OK. Could not verify the actual DSN value (not present in committed code or `.env`). Just confirm it is a project DSN, not an internal organization auth token or `SENTRY_AUTH_TOKEN` — those are read-write and must never have a `NEXT_PUBLIC_` prefix. Vercel env-var dashboard is the place to verify.
impact: If DSN is a regular Sentry public DSN: zero impact (by design). If somebody pasted an internal auth token by mistake: attacker can read all error data and modify project settings.
recommendation: Open Vercel env vars → check `NEXT_PUBLIC_SENTRY_DSN` value starts with `https://<publicKey>@<orgIngest>.ingest.sentry.io/<projectId>` and has no `auth_token` style segment. Also confirm `SENTRY_AUTH_TOKEN` (used at build time for sourcemap upload) is NOT prefixed `NEXT_PUBLIC_`.

title: Open redirect via attacker-controlled notification.link to phishing site
description: The notifications-bell calls `router.push(n.link)` directly with a string sourced from the `notifications.link` column. That column is populated by `POST /api/notifications/internal` (src/app/api/notifications/internal/route.ts:89) which only requires the shared `SP_NOTIFICATIONS_SECRET` header and accepts `body.link ?? null` with NO validation at all (no scheme check, no host check, no length cap). Anyone who learns the webhook secret (it is shared across the Python reclamation webhook + Bitrix integration and noted in MEMORY.md as `app-password у git` / `treba rotate`) can inject notifications with `link: "https://evil.com/login"` or `link: "javascript:fetch('/api/...')"`. Next.js `router.push` accepts external URLs and `javascript:` URIs depending on version, and at minimum the call passes the value straight into navigation, enabling phishing redirects on click of a real-looking in-app notification.
impact: Account takeover via phishing — manager clicks a notification, lands on attacker page that mimics the EMET login, captures credentials. Also enables javascript: scheme XSS if Next.js does not strip it.
recommendation: In `/api/notifications/internal` validate `link`: must be a string, start with `/`, not start with `//`, length < 256, and must NOT contain `:` before the first `/` (rejects `javascript:`, `data:`, absolute URLs). Mirror this guard client-side in `handleClickItem`: `if (!n.link.startsWith('/') || n.link.startsWith('//')) return;` before calling `router.push`.

title: Notification title/message stored without length cap — DoS + DB bloat
description: `POST /api/notifications/internal` trims `title` and accepts `message` and `meta` (a JSON object) with no length or size bounds. The row is then inserted into Supabase. An attacker with the webhook secret (or a buggy upstream) can post a 10MB `title` / `message` / `meta`, repeated thousands of times — bloating the `notifications` table, slowing the bell endpoint for every user, and bypassing the Supabase Free tier limits. Also: `request.json()` itself has no explicit body-size limit in this route, so the route handler will buffer arbitrarily large payloads into memory before the validator runs.
impact: Storage exhaustion on Supabase Pro, slow notification loads (the bell `select *` returns all 50 most recent for each user — bloated rows = slow UI), potential memory pressure on Vercel function from oversize JSON parse.
recommendation: Cap inputs explicitly before insert: `title.slice(0, 200)`, `message?.slice(0, 1000)`, `JSON.stringify(meta).length > 4096 -> reject`. Also early-reject body using `Content-Length` header check (`if (Number(request.headers.get('content-length')) > 8192) return 413`).

title: XSS via dangerouslySetInnerHTML — hand-rolled HTML sanitizer is bypassable
description:
`sanitizeCommentHtml` is a regex-based stripper that only removes ``, ``, and attributes matching `on\w+="..."` / `on\w+='...'`. It misses many vectors: (1) `on*` handlers without quotes (``), (2) ``, `
` etc., (3) `javascript:` URIs in `` and ``, (4) malformed tags like `ipt>` that survive a single regex pass and then get "unfolded" by the browser, (5) `
impact: Stored XSS — a malicious Bitrix comment runs JS in the manager's browser, can call `/api/onec` proxy on their behalf, exfiltrate client data, or escalate to admin if an admin views the same claim.
recommendation: Replace the hand-rolled regex with `DOMPurify` (`isomorphic-dompurify` works in Next.js): `DOMPurify.sanitize(html, { ALLOWED_TAGS: ['b','i','u','br','a','p','strong','em'], ALLOWED_ATTR: ['href'], ALLOWED_URI_REGEXP: /^(https?|mailto):/i })`. The footprint is ~20KB and closes every documented bypass.

title: Path traversal / SSRF via attacker-controlled bitrixUrl absolute path
description: When Bitrix Disk lookup fails, the route falls back to `request.nextUrl.searchParams.get('bitrixUrl')`. If the value starts with `/` it prepends `https://${host}` and fetches it from the Bitrix host. The host check via `new URL(absolute).host !== host` correctly blocks cross-host abuse, but it does NOT validate the path: an attacker can pass `?bitrixUrl=/rest/user.get?auth=...` or `?bitrixUrl=/bitrix/admin/security_session_list.php` to make the server fetch arbitrary internal Bitrix endpoints using the server's network position. The fetched bytes are then streamed back to the user with `Content-Type` from the Bitrix response, effectively giving the user a proxy to any Bitrix URL on the same host (including admin pages if Bitrix trusts the server IP).
impact: SSRF inside Bitrix — manager can read internal Bitrix admin endpoints, REST methods, or session lists via the proxy. Combined with the implicit trust Bitrix gives the EMET app server, this could leak credentials/tokens.
recommendation: Restrict allowed paths: only accept `bitrixUrl` whose pathname starts with `/upload/` or `/download/` (Bitrix file-storage prefixes) and rejects query strings that contain `auth`, `token`, or `sessid`. Better: drop the bitrixUrl fallback entirely and require the Disk API path.

title: 1С proxy logs Basic Auth body and full payload (credential leakage in Vercel logs)
description: `console.log('[ШАГ 1] Відправка в 1С...', JSON.stringify({ action, payload: safePayload }))` dumps the full payload to Vercel logs on every 1С call. For `saveClientSurvey` and `registerNewClient` the payload includes raw client PII (phone, address, education docs, files metadata). For `getInitialData` it includes full meeting lists. Vercel log retention is 1 month on Pro plan and any engineer with project access can read it. The same pattern truncates the response at 1000 chars but still logs the rest of the request unredacted. There is no PII redaction.
impact: GDPR/PD compliance risk — client phone numbers, names, and document metadata accumulate in Vercel logs accessible to anyone with project read access. If the project is ever shared (preview deploys, team additions), PII leaks downstream.
recommendation: Add a redaction helper: for `saveClientSurvey`/`registerNewClient` log only `{action, fields: Object.keys(payload)}`. For all other actions log `{action, login: payload.login}` and skip the rest. Or use `process.env.NODE_ENV !== 'production'` gate so the verbose log only runs in dev.

title: PostgREST column name not validated in eq/in/notIn — second-order injection risk
description: Functions `eq(column, value)`, `is(column, value)`, `lt(column, value)`, `in(column, values)`, `notIn(column, values)` URL-encode the *value* but the `column` parameter is concatenated raw into the query string (`${column}=eq.${...}`). All current call sites pass constant strings, so it is not exploited TODAY — but it is a foot-gun: any future code path that passes a user-controlled field name (e.g. dynamic filter UI) immediately turns into a PostgREST query injection (`column="user_id=eq.X&password=eq.Y"` etc.). The escape comment in this file boasts about value escaping but never addresses the column name. Same applies to `select(columns)` — if a caller passes user input as `select` columns, an attacker can read arbitrary columns including hashes/secrets.
impact: Future regression — first developer to wire a dynamic sort/filter column from query params introduces an authenticated query-injection that reads or modifies arbitrary rows.
recommendation: In each method validate column against `/^[a-z_][a-z0-9_]*$/i.test(column)` and throw on mismatch. Same for `select` columns (allow `*`, `,`, `(`, `)`, and the identifier regex).

title: No body-size limit on /api/onec proxy — DoS via large payload
description: `POST /api/onec` calls `await request.json()` without checking `Content-Length` or capping body size. A logged-in manager (or stolen session cookie) can post a 100MB JSON `payload` — the Vercel function buffers it into memory, then forwards it to 1С. The rate limiter caps at 60 req/min but does NOT cap per-request size, so a single burst of 10×100MB requests within rate limit can OOM the function or saturate the 1С upstream. Same pattern in `/api/meetings`, `/api/planning`, `/api/notifications/internal` — none of them check size.
impact: Easy authenticated DoS against the 1С proxy and against Vercel function memory budget. 1С is single-node and can be downed for the whole company by one logged-in user.
recommendation: Early-reject by Content-Length header in each POST route: `const len = Number(request.headers.get('content-length') ?? 0); if (len > 64_000) return Response.json({error:'body too large'}, {status:413});`. For `/api/onec`, 64KB is plenty (largest legitimate payload is `getClientFocus` with ~200 IDs ≈ 6KB).

title: Meeting comment / purpose accepted without length cap — stored bloat + UI break
description: POST /api/meetings reads `body.comment` and `body.purpose` and passes them straight to `createMeeting`. There are no length checks — a manager (or compromised session) can save a 10MB `comment`. Same for `plannedAddress`. These fields are then loaded in bulk by `/api/meetings` GET (limit 500) and rendered in UI. A few 10MB comments will hang the meetings page in the browser.
impact: Either accidental (paste a giant log into the comment field) or malicious bloat slows down `/meetings` for every manager in the same region (RM scope='managed'). Supabase row size also has hard limits.
recommendation: Cap before insert: `comment: (body.comment ?? '').slice(0, 2000) || null`, `purpose: (body.purpose ?? '').slice(0, 200) || null`, `plannedAddress: ... .slice(0, 500) || null`. Same audit for `claim-form-dialog` description and `saveClientSurvey` payload fields.

title: Form labels not programmatically associated with inputs (login + meeting form + claim form)
description: Throughout the app, form fields use either bare <label> elements as siblings of inputs (login-form.tsx lines 72/83), or a FormGroup wrapper where <label> is a sibling of children rather than wrapping them (meeting-form.tsx FormGroup at line 422 — `<label>{label}</label>{children}`). The claim-form-dialog repeats the same pattern (lines 268-365: <label> followed by sibling <select>/<input>). A grep over src/components returned ZERO occurrences of `htmlFor`. Without `htmlFor`+`id` (or genuine nesting), screen readers do not announce the label when focus lands on the input, and users cannot click the label to focus the field. This breaks WCAG 1.3.1 Info and Relationships and 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value across Email, Password, Дата, Час, Тривалість, Мета візиту, Клієнт, Тип скарги, Препарат, LOT, № Реалізації, anketa fields, тощо.
impact: Screen-reader users (VoiceOver, NVDA, JAWS) hear only the input type with no field name on login, meeting creation, and reclamation submission. Tapping the visual label does not focus the input — degrades motor-impaired UX. Affects every form in the app, including the new Sprint 2B reclamation flow.
recommendation: Either (a) wrap the input INSIDE the <label> element (implicit association), or (b) generate a stable id (React.useId()) and set `htmlFor={id}` on the label plus `id={id}` on the input. Refactor FormGroup to accept an id and inject it into children via React.cloneElement, or expose a render-prop `({ id }) => <input id={id}/>`. Fix login-form lines 72-91 the same way.

title: Dialog/Sheet close button uses size-7 (28px) — below 44px tap target
description: DialogContent renders the close X with `<Button variant="ghost" size="icon-sm" />`. Per button.tsx line 33-34, size `icon-sm` resolves to `size-7` (Tailwind 28×28 px). Sheet.tsx line 69 uses the same `icon-sm`. The base `icon` size is `size-8` (32 px) and `icon-xs` is `size-6` (24 px) — none of the icon-button sizes in the Button variants meet the 44 px target the rest of the app enforces (e.g. meeting-card phone/claim buttons explicitly use `w-11 h-11`). Every shadcn Dialog used in clients/meetings/claims inherits this 28 px close button, contradicting the project memory rule `ui_no_emojis_44px_taps` that mobile action buttons should be ≥44 px.
impact: On touch devices, closing modals (NewClientDialog, GlobalClientSearchDialog, ConfirmDialog, AttachmentLightbox, custom-month-picker popover, anything built on shadcn Dialog/Sheet) requires hitting a 28 px target — fails Apple HIG and Android Material 48 dp guidance, and WCAG 2.5.5 Target Size (Enhanced, AAA) plus the new 2.5.8 Target Size (Minimum, AA, 24 px minimum which it barely passes for icon-sm but icon-xs at 24 px is on the edge).
recommendation: Change `size="icon-sm"` → `size="icon"` plus a custom class bumping to `w-11 h-11` for dialogs, OR add a new `icon-touch` variant at 44 px and use it for all close/X buttons in Dialog/Sheet/custom-month-picker. The hand-rolled dialogs in claim-form-dialog.tsx (line 238) and meeting-form.tsx (line 228) already use `w-11 h-11` — port that to the shared primitive.

title: LoadingScreen has no aria-live region — screen readers do not announce loading state
description: LoadingScreen renders a spinner + label inside a plain `<div>` with no `role="status"`, `aria-live`, or `aria-busy`. A grep over the entire `src/` tree returned zero `aria-live` matches. The component is the primary loading state for the clients page (`<LoadingScreen label="Завантажуємо клієнтів…" />` at clients-page.tsx line 523), claims pages, meetings, and is shown for several seconds while 1С Action 5/Action 4 are cold-starting (the auto-reload guard in clients-page line 320 waits 30 s). The Loader2 SVG is decorative-only and there is no announcement that content is loading or has loaded.
impact: WCAG 4.1.3 Status Messages: assistive-tech users (and many users with cognitive disabilities relying on screen-reader feedback) get no signal that the app is busy. They may attempt to navigate, hear silence, and assume the page is broken. With 1С cold-start delays of 5–30 s, this is a real and repeated UX failure.
recommendation: Add `role="status"` and `aria-live="polite"` to the LoadingScreen root, e.g. `<div role="status" aria-live="polite" aria-busy="true" className=…>`. Wrap the Loader2 in `<span aria-hidden="true">` and ensure the visible label text is the accessible name. Apply the same pattern to the inline toast in clients-page.tsx line 838-844 (currently no live region, so success/error toasts go silent for screen readers).

title: Animated spinners and pulses ignore prefers-reduced-motion
description: globals.css gates `.glass-card`, `.pulse-dot`, and `.fade-stagger` on `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)` (lines 178, 279, 307). However Tailwind's `animate-spin`, `animate-pulse`, and `animate-bounce` are used in 13+ places (LoadingScreen Loader2 line 22, login-form spinner line 113, clients-page registry-loading skeleton lines 1013-1017 with animate-pulse, HeroVykonannya pulse dots, LiveTimer dot in meeting-card.tsx line 549, etc.) without a reduced-motion override. Tailwind's defaults do not respect the media query; you must opt in via `motion-reduce:animate-none` or extend the config.
impact: WCAG 2.3.3 Animation from Interactions (AAA) and the user-comfort guidance under 2.2.2 Pause, Stop, Hide. Users with vestibular disorders who enable `prefers-reduced-motion: reduce` still see endless spinning and pulsing during 1С cold-starts. This is the type of motion most likely to trigger discomfort because it loops continuously.
recommendation: Either (a) add `motion-reduce:animate-none` next to every `animate-spin`/`animate-pulse` usage, or (b) globally override in globals.css under the existing `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)` block: `.animate-spin, .animate-pulse, .animate-bounce { animation: none !important; }`. The second is one line and covers all current and future usages.

title: FilterPill buttons in clients page sticky bar fall well under 44 px touch target
description: The ClientsMonthFilter pill group (lines 957-963) is a 36 px tall row of 28 px (`h-7 px-3`) buttons, sitting in a sticky position under the header. The category filter pills below (FilterPill component used at lines 681-692) follow the same pattern. These are the primary navigation between months and client categories on the page — they are not secondary chrome. The memory rule `ui_no_emojis_44px_taps` explicitly mandates ≥44 px for mobile action buttons.
impact: WCAG 2.5.8 Target Size (Minimum, AA — 24 px) is met but 2.5.5 (AAA, 44 px) is not, and Apple HIG/Material Design both flag 28 px tap targets as fat-finger error-prone. Users mis-tap month filters on phones, switching to the wrong period and seeing stale data. The memory note `large_commits_break_prod` shows this category of UX paper-cut is what users report after release.
recommendation: Bump pill height to at least `h-9` (36 px) on desktop and `min-h-[44px]` on `max-md:`. The current `inline-flex items-center h-7 px-3` becomes `inline-flex items-center h-9 max-md:min-h-[44px] px-3`. Same change for FilterPill — find its definition and increase to 44 px on mobile.

title: muted-foreground #7c879a on glass-card background fails WCAG AA contrast for small text
description: `--muted-foreground: #7c879a` (line 84) provides ~4.05:1 contrast against pure white (#fff) — just below the WCAG AA 4.5:1 threshold for text under 18 pt. The token is applied to text rendered at 10-12 px in many places: clients-page line 866 (`text-[12px] text-muted-foreground`), HeroVykonannya line 1011 (`text-[10px] uppercase tracking-wider text-muted-foreground`), card subtitles, hint text, sub-labels. Critically, these are rendered ON the `.glass-card` background, which is `rgba(255,255,255,0.55)` over a light blue/green mesh — so the effective contrast drops further (closer to 3.5:1). Sheet.tsx description (line 123) and dialog.tsx description (line 141) also default to this color.
impact: WCAG 1.4.3 Contrast (Minimum) AA failure for sub-12 px text on glass surfaces. Low-vision users and anyone on a daylight phone screen struggles to read the date subtitle, hint text under inputs, hero card eyebrow labels, and counter values. With the design system using glass-card extensively and 343 occurrences of `text-muted-foreground` across 30 component files, this is the single highest-volume contrast issue.
recommendation: Darken `--muted-foreground` from #7c879a to about #5d6678 (≈5.5:1 vs white, still passes vs glass). This is a single token change in globals.css :root. Re-run a contrast checker against `.glass-card` and `.glass-card-flat` backgrounds. Alternatively introduce `--muted-foreground-strong` for text ≤12 px and use it in eyebrow labels and hints.

title: Notifications dropdown close button is 28 px and the dropdown traps no focus
description: The Bell button itself is `w-9 h-9` (36 px — already under 44 px), and the dropdown close X inside is `w-7 h-7` (28 px) at line 162-169. The dropdown is opened via React state with manual outside-click + Escape handlers (lines 49-65) instead of a real Dialog/Menu primitive. As a result: (1) when open, Tab navigation flows out of the dropdown into background content; (2) focus is not moved into the dropdown on open and is not returned to the bell button on close; (3) there is no `role="menu"` or `aria-expanded` on the trigger to announce the open/closed state.
impact: Keyboard and screen-reader users cannot reliably operate notifications: they tab into the page behind the dropdown, lose track of which notification has focus, and the trigger button does not announce its expanded state. The 28/36 px tap targets compound the mobile-touch issue.
recommendation: Either rebuild the dropdown on `@base-ui/react/popover` (already a dependency, used by other components) which provides focus management + Escape + outside-click + `aria-expanded` out of the box, or manually add `aria-expanded={open}` and `aria-haspopup="menu"` to the Bell button (line 121), set `role="menu"` on the dropdown, focus the first interactive item on open, and return focus to the Bell on close. Bump both buttons to `w-11 h-11`.

title: Hard-coded fallback SESSION_SECRET allows JWT forgery on any non-production deploy
description: getSecret() falls through to the literal string 'dev-only-secret-change-in-production-32b' whenever process.env.NODE_ENV !== 'production'. The validation block (lines 30-45) only enforces presence/length when NODE_ENV === 'production'. On Vercel Preview/Staging deployments, on the `dev` environment, or on any deploy where NODE_ENV is mis-set (e.g. 'development', 'staging', undefined at runtime in an edge worker), every JWT is signed with a constant that lives in the public git history.
impact: Anyone who reads the source (it's a public repo path) can forge a valid sp_session cookie for ANY login by signing {login,fullName,role,region,regionCode,managedUsers} with HS256 + that constant. Full account takeover including director/admin roles on preview URLs, which often share Supabase prod data.
recommendation: Throw unconditionally when SESSION_SECRET is missing or <32 bytes, regardless of NODE_ENV. For local dev, require devs to set SESSION_SECRET in .env.local — there is no scenario where a baked-in secret is acceptable.

title: validateApiRequest allow-all in any non-production environment
description: After Sec-Fetch-Site and Origin checks fail, the function returns { valid: true } whenever process.env.NODE_ENV !== 'production'. There is no API key requirement, no Origin requirement — nothing. Combined with the fact that Vercel Preview deployments do NOT necessarily run with NODE_ENV='production' for every workflow (e.g. branch deploys triggered via vercel CLI with --target preview without overriding NODE_ENV, or any self-hosted env), every /api/* endpoint on those URLs accepts unauthenticated cross-origin requests.
impact: Attacker pointing curl/fetch with `Origin: https://evil.com` (or no Origin at all) at the preview URL of any branch passes all checks → can call /api/onec proxy, /api/admin/*, /api/clients-cache/* without credentials. If the preview also reads from prod Supabase (common pattern), they get full prod data exfiltration.
recommendation: Remove the env-based bypass entirely. For local development, set up a known-good API key in .env.local. Production-style auth in all environments avoids the 'preview is leaky' class of bugs.

title: x-api-key compared with !== (timing oracle)
description: External integrations are authenticated by `authHeader !== getApiSecret()`. JavaScript string equality is not constant-time — it short-circuits at the first mismatched byte. Over enough samples an attacker on a low-latency network (or co-located on the same Vercel region) can statistically infer each byte of API_SECRET_KEY.
impact: Slow but real: byte-by-byte recovery of API_SECRET_KEY. Once known, attacker can call any /api/* route with x-api-key from any origin, bypassing all Sec-Fetch/Origin gates.
recommendation: Use crypto.timingSafeEqual on equal-length Buffers (compare hashes, e.g. sha256 of both sides, then timingSafeEqual on those digests so length doesn't leak either).

title: Per-IP login rate-limit allows unlimited password guesses against a target login via IP rotation
description: checkRateLimit key is `login:${ip}` — bound to the source IP, not to the username. There is no per-login lockout or counter. An attacker rotating residential IPs (or using Tor, or a small botnet) can submit thousands of password attempts against `sdu@emet.in.ua` (director) without ever tripping the limiter, because each IP gets its own 60/min × 600/hr budget. There is also no exponential backoff and no notification on N failed attempts.
impact: Director and admin accounts are reachable by online brute-force / credential-stuffing. With director_credentials.md noting the director account exists at a known email, this is the highest-value target. 1С doesn't necessarily lock accounts after N failures either.
recommendation: Add a SECOND rate-limit keyed by `login:${login.toLowerCase()}` (e.g. 10/min, 50/hr). Lock the account (return 423) after 20 failed attempts in 1h regardless of IP. Log failed attempts to Supabase for monitoring.

title: In-memory rate limiter is per-instance — effective limit scales with number of warm Lambdas
description: The `buckets` Map lives in process memory. Vercel runs N concurrent function instances (often 5-20 under load). Each instance independently allows 60 req/min, so the real ceiling is N × 60/min for the same IP. An attacker can deliberately keep multiple instances warm and burst across them.
impact: Brute-force on /api/auth/login can hit ~hundreds of attempts/minute against the same login from a single IP — already enough to break weak passwords. The comment acknowledges this risk but no fix shipped.
recommendation: Move to Upstash Redis or Vercel KV with @upstash/ratelimit. Free tier covers this app's volume. Until then, lower MAX_PER_WINDOW for `login:*` keys to something like 10/min so even 10 instances cap at 100/min.

title: 1С upstream error message forwarded to client — enables user enumeration
description: `json.message || 'Невірний логін або пароль'` returns whatever string 1С put in the error response. 1С commonly distinguishes 'Користувача не знайдено' vs 'Невірний пароль' vs 'Користувач заблокований' — and forwards those verbatim. Frontend then displays the upstream message.
impact: Attacker can enumerate valid logins (knowing which @emet.in.ua addresses exist) before brute-forcing. Combined with finding #4 (no per-login lockout), turns a closed system into an open enumeration target. Also potentially leaks 1С infra hints (Module(2146) etc.) the way Action 5 errors do (per memory).
recommendation: Never echo `json.message` for login errors. Hardcode 'Невірний логін або пароль' regardless of what 1С said. Log the upstream detail server-side only.

title: JWT carries role/managedUsers/region for 24h with no revocation — admin demotion or region change takes up to a day
description: TTL_SECONDS = 86400 and the JWT payload (lines 62-69) freezes role, region, regionCode, managedUsers at sign time. /api/auth/me refreshes only can_edit_stages_after_finalize, can_view_company_overview, can_unfinalize_plans from Supabase — it does NOT re-read role or managedUsers. Logout just deletes the cookie; a leaked JWT remains valid for the full 24h since there is no jti/revocation list.
impact: Scenarios: (a) director fires a manager who keeps an open tab → that manager retains role/managedUsers access for up to 24h. (b) JWT leaked via any XSS-equivalent or log harvest → attacker keeps director access for 24h with no kill switch. (c) Region reassignment in 1С doesn't propagate until next login.
recommendation: Either: read role/managedUsers fresh from Supabase in getSession() (one extra SELECT, already done for permission flags), or shorten TTL to 1-2h with silent refresh, or add a session_version column in users that increments on demote and gate verify on match.

title: Sec-Fetch-Site 'same-site' accepted unconditionally allows write-CSRF from sibling vercel.app preview origins
description: The check accepts both 'same-origin' AND 'same-site' for all methods including POST/PATCH/DELETE. sameSite=Lax on the sp_session cookie + Sec-Fetch-Site=same-site means a different host on the same registrable domain can carry the cookie. The cookie domain isn't explicitly scoped so it defaults to the exact host (sales-planning-lyart.vercel.app), which is what saves this from being critical — but if anyone ever sets cookie Domain=.vercel.app, or moves to a custom apex domain with subdomains, every neighbor becomes a CSRF vector.
impact: Today: low because vercel.app is on Mozilla PSL so 'same-site' degenerates to 'cross-site' for sibling vercel.app hosts. After moving to a custom domain like emet.in.ua with subdomains (likely), any compromised subdomain can POST to /api/* with the manager's cookie attached.
recommendation: For write methods, accept ONLY 'same-origin'. Keep 'same-site' for GET only (mirroring the existing 'none' write-block pattern). Also explicitly set the cookie's Domain= attribute to the exact host when you move to a custom domain, and consider SameSite=Strict for sp_session.

title: Core tables (users/forecasts/gap_closures/period_summaries/periods/planning_*) have NO RLS — only meetings/notifications protected
