description: Only `meetings`, `meeting_syncs`, and `notifications` have `ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY`. The core planning tables — `users` (with role/password_hash), `forecasts`, `gap_closures`, `period_summaries`, `periods`, `planning_snapshots`, `planning_locks`, `planning_settings` — have NO RLS at all. The trust model is entirely 'service_role + ручний filter manager_login=session.login у /api/*'. Anyone who obtains the anon/publishable Supabase key (and `NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL` is already public) could read every manager's plan, every user record, password hashes if they exist, and every snapshot history.
impact: Defense-in-depth completely missing for the most sensitive tables. If anon key is accidentally exposed (e.g. someone installs @supabase/supabase-js on the client per the explicit warning in CLAUDE.md), all planning data + users table become readable/writable. The safety-net rationale in migration 017 (defense-in-depth against a future leak) was explicitly applied to meetings but never to the rest.
recommendation: Apply the same `ALTER TABLE ... ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;` default-deny pattern from 017 to: users, forecasts, gap_closures, period_summaries, periods, planning_snapshots, planning_locks, planning_settings. Service role continues to bypass — backend code keeps working. This is one short migration with zero behavioral risk.

title: notifications.user_login has no FK to users — orphan rows survive user deletion
description: `notifications.user_login text not null` is a free-form text column with no `REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE/RESTRICT`. The same gap exists in `planning_locks.user_login` (migration 010, line 31). By contrast, `meetings.manager_login` (013) correctly has `REFERENCES users(login) ON DELETE RESTRICT`. Typos at insert time will silently succeed; deleting a user leaves orphan notifications/locks that no UI surface owns.
impact: Phantom notifications keep accumulating for deactivated managers (memory says users are real, e.g. trial managers come and go). The bell dropdown can list rows for a user that no longer exists, and admin cannot delete a user cleanly because no constraint forces cleanup. dedup_key still protects against duplicates, but referential integrity is lost.
recommendation: Add FK in next migration: `ALTER TABLE notifications ADD CONSTRAINT notifications_user_login_fkey FOREIGN KEY (user_login) REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE;` and same for planning_locks.user_login. Backfill: run a one-shot DELETE for rows whose user_login is not in users first.

title: No CHECK constraint on users.role — DB accepts any string, contradicts code's closed set
description: Grep across the entire supabase/migrations tree finds zero `CHECK (role IN (...))` constraint on users. The code treats role as a closed enum ('manager','rm','director','admin'), and `meetings_schema.sql` policies hard-code `current_setting('app.role') IN ('director','admin')`. But the column itself accepts any string — an admin UPDATE typo like role='Admin' or role='direcot' breaks authorization silently. Same gap on `notifications.type` (021 line 18) — comment explicitly says 'valid types checked in TypeScript' (i.e. trusted, not enforced).
impact: Role-based access control depends entirely on string matching. A misspelled role bypasses no-permission paths (e.g. is not 'admin' so admin checks fail closed) but breaks legitimate user access silently. In the future RLS migration (planned in README M5) this becomes load-bearing — wrong role values will make policies behave incorrectly.
recommendation: Add `ALTER TABLE users ADD CONSTRAINT users_role_chk CHECK (role IN ('manager','rm','director','admin'));` and matching constraint on `meetings.status` already exists as example. For notifications.type, document allowed values OR add a CHECK if the set is stable.

title: notifications dedup_key UNIQUE is too broad — different external systems collide on same key string
description: The dedup uniqueness is `UNIQUE (dedup_key) WHERE dedup_key IS NOT NULL` — a single global namespace. Comment suggests format `bitrix:claim:12:comment:9876`, but anything sourcing notifications (Bitrix, 1С, future Telegram, internal meetings reminders) shares one keyspace. If two sources accidentally pick the same template (`claim:12:comment:9876`), the second legitimate notification silently fails with 23505 conflict and the user never sees it. Worse: the index does not include user_login, so an event meant for user A with the same dedup_key as user B will be rejected even though they are different recipients.
impact: Silent notification loss. Hard to debug because dedup_key collisions don't surface in UI — Bitrix webhook gets a 'duplicate' error, retry logic considers it 'already delivered', user never gets the bell ping. Cross-user dedup collision is particularly nasty for shared reclamation threads where the same comment-id should generate a notification per subscriber.
recommendation: Either namespace dedup_key by prefix convention in code (and add a CHECK that it starts with a known source prefix), or make the unique index composite: `UNIQUE (user_login, dedup_key) WHERE dedup_key IS NOT NULL`. The latter is the most robust.

title: RLS policies in migration 013 rely on current_setting('app.login') but service_role bypasses — policies are dead code never exercised
description: Migration 013 creates 4+4 RLS policies on meetings/meeting_syncs using `current_setting('app.login', true)`. Migration 017 (safety net) then explicitly relies on default-deny instead. The 013 policies are never actually used: the backend uses service_role (bypasses RLS) and the frontend has no direct Supabase access. The `app.login` GUC is never set anywhere in code — grep confirms no `SET LOCAL app.login` in the codebase. When RLS is eventually enforced for real (README M5), these policies will be tested for the first time in production.
impact: False sense of security in code review — 'we have RLS, look at the policies' — when in fact they have never been exercised even once. The 'switch on' moment will be a new code path tested in prod. Also, every policy adds query planner overhead and review surface for zero current benefit.
recommendation: Either (a) write an integration test that creates a non-service-role JWT, SETs app.login, and verifies the policies actually filter, or (b) replace the policies with the same default-deny pattern as 017 and re-design them properly when the JWT auth migration starts. Document the trust model in CLAUDE.md or supabase/README.md.

title: planning_snapshots has no index on (period_id, user_id, segment_code, block_type) for hot read path
description: planning_snapshots has UNIQUE (period_id, user_id, segment_code, block_type, client_id_1c) which serves as an index, plus idx_planning_snapshots_period_user_segment (period_id, user_id, segment_code) and idx_planning_snapshots_period (period_id). After migration 007 consolidated to monthly pids, the table reached ~19,901 rows. Queries that filter by block_type ('forecast' vs 'gap') without client_id_1c likely scan the segment-level index then filter — fine at current size, but no covering index on the block_type predicate. Lower priority but worth noting since memory mentions this table is heavily used for the 'who was in the initial list' audit.
impact: At today's row counts (~20k) the planner handles this fine. As planning_snapshots accumulates monthly (every snapshot persists forever — no archive cleanup), this becomes a slow point in 6-12 months. Not urgent but on the radar.
recommendation: Either (a) add `CREATE INDEX idx_planning_snapshots_per_user_seg_type ON planning_snapshots (period_id, user_id, segment_code, block_type);` when the audit feature is exercised more, or (b) add a TTL/archive policy for periods older than e.g. 12 months so the table stays bounded. No immediate action required.

title: finalize POST has a TOCTOU race: SELECT-then-UPSERT not atomic, double-fire corrupts finalized_by audit
description: POST /api/planning/finalize first SELECTs period_summaries for an existing finalized_at, then UPSERTs if null. Between the SELECT and the UPSERT there is no row-level lock. Two concurrent finalize requests (e.g., double-click, or admin + RM hitting the same manager-segment-period at the same time) BOTH observe existing=null, BOTH write finalized_at=NOW() and finalized_by=session.login via UPSERT with onConflict='period_id,user_id,segment_code'. Last writer wins on finalized_at and finalized_by — but neither got the 'alreadyFinalized: true' short-circuit, so both clients believe THEY finalized first.
impact: Audit log of who finalized a plan can be silently overwritten. If an RM and the manager both finalize in the same instant (common pattern: manager hits save+finalize, RM also clicks finalize on the dashboard), the recorded finalized_by may not match what either UI shows. Equality-of-clock tie-breaker is non-deterministic. Recovery requires manual SQL inspection because no rollback mechanism exists.
recommendation: Replace SELECT+UPSERT with a single PATCH …?period_id=eq.X&user_id=eq.Y&segment_code=eq.Z&finalized_at=is.null (CAS: only-if-not-already-finalized), and inspect rows-affected via Prefer: return=representation. If 0 rows updated, do a second SELECT to fetch the existing finalized_at/finalized_by and return alreadyFinalized=true. This makes the operation idempotent under concurrency without serialising at the application layer.

title: Optimistic notification mark-read never rolls back on server reject (HTTP 4xx/5xx)
description: markRead() and markAllRead() optimistically update the SWR cache then `await fetch(...)`. The catch branch (which re-fetches to roll back) only runs on network errors / thrown exceptions. A 401, 403, 404, or 500 response from /api/notifications/[id]/read or /mark-all-read is silently swallowed — fetch resolves normally, the optimistic state stays, and the unread badge shows 0 even though the database row was never updated.
impact: User sees notification as read; on refresh / next poll cycle (30s) the unread badge jumps back, but inside that window critical alerts (claim_status_changed, meeting_reminder) can be acknowledged-from-UI without ever being persisted. For mark-all-read this can hide dozens of unread items until the next polling tick. On a stale/expired session every notification click silently 'reads' nothing.
recommendation: Check `r.ok` after the fetch. If not ok, call `mutate()` (with revalidate) to discard optimistic state, and surface a toast. Same fix in both markRead and markAllRead.

title: archive POST deletes periods/forecasts/gap_closures but leaves planning_snapshots orphaned
description: Archive copies forecasts, gap_closures and period_summaries into archive_* tables, then deletes them plus the periods rows. planning_snapshots (which references period_id via the same scheme) is never copied and never deleted. After the run, planning_snapshots rows for archived periods reference a period_id whose `periods` row no longer exists. Either FK constraint will block the delete (silently surfaced as 'Delete periods' error in delErrors only), or — if no FK — snapshots silently become dangling. Same gap exists for users that have only old rows: their last_purchase derived state lives in snapshots forever.
impact: Either archive fails halfway (delete-phase error, archive table populated, originals partially gone, snapshots intact -> next run double-inserts into archive tables), or DB integrity reference rot — snapshots reference period_id with no parent row, making any join-based audit return empty/broken data. Backfill scripts that rely on snapshots for 'state at start of month' return wrong/missing data after archive.
recommendation: Either (a) include planning_snapshots in the archive: copy to archive_planning_snapshots, then delete by period_id ∈ ids — same pattern as the others; or (b) explicitly delete planning_snapshots for those period_ids and document that historical-snapshot recovery is lost on archive. Add a test that runs archive twice in a row to catch FK/orphan regressions.

title: Window-lock and finalized state are checked but auto-reviving archived_at=null on every save resurrects soft-deleted rows
description: In planning POST, every forecast and gap row produced by the request is written with archived_at=null (lines 321 and 341), and the UPSERT key is (period_id, user_id, segment_code, client_id_1c). If a client was soft-archived (archived_at NOT NULL) by M8 admin tooling, a subsequent client-side save that happens to include that client_id_1c — even a stale autosave from a tab that loaded before archive — will UPSERT-merge archived_at back to NULL, silently un-archiving the row. GET filters by `is('archived_at', null)`, so the client may not even know the archived row exists when it sends the body.
impact: Audit / soft-delete guarantees are weak: anything an admin or migration archives can be silently revived by any concurrent or stale-tab save. This defeats M8 'we keep archived rows for audit / safe revert'. On a re-imported plan from 1С the unintended revival is invisible (no log entry).
recommendation: Don't blindly set archived_at=null on every upsert. Either (a) omit archived_at from the upsert payload entirely (PostgREST merge-duplicates won't overwrite columns not present), or (b) explicit two-step: SELECT existing archived_at; only set null when caller explicitly opts in (e.g. body.reviveArchived=true). Add server-side log when a revival happens.

title: user_id is the raw session login with no case/whitespace normalization — silent data silos and ACL bypass risk
description: planning POST and finalize POST use `effectiveLogin` directly as user_id for UPSERTs into forecasts/gap_closures/period_summaries/users (planning route line 173 `const uid = effectiveLogin;`, finalize line 121). There is no `.toLowerCase().trim()` like /api/planning/aggregate does (route.ts line 70) and /api/clients/plan-totals does. The ACL check `session.managedUsers.includes(effectiveLogin)` is also case-sensitive. Result: if an RM hits `/api/planning?login=Manager@Emet.in.ua` (capitalised) but managedUsers contains `manager@emet.in.ua`, the include() returns false and the request is 403'd; OR if the caller passes a casing variant that DOES match managedUsers but differs from the casing under which rows already exist in DB, all UPSERTs land in a parallel 'shadow' user row with `id='Manager@Emet.in.ua'`, while reads via dashboard normalise to lowercase and never see them.
impact: Two failure modes in production: (1) reports/dashboards (aggregate, plan-totals) lowercase user_id when querying — saved data under uppercase login is invisible in those views; (2) ON CONFLICT on (period_id, user_id, segment_code, client_id_1c) treats `Foo@x` and `foo@x` as DIFFERENT rows, so the same client appears twice (one finalized, one draft), and Б.6 finalizedSet has the wrong key. Also, planning GET (line 57) allows drill-down using `session.managedUsers.includes(requestedLogin)` exact-match; if managedUsers and the URL param normalize differently per code path, there is a small ACL bypass window for sibling managers with case-equivalent logins (rare but real).
recommendation: Centralise login normalization in `getSession()` so session.login and session.managedUsers are ALREADY lowercased+trimmed, and apply the same `.toLowerCase().trim()` to every `targetLogin`/`requestedLogin` body/query parameter before scope check and before using as user_id. Add a CHECK constraint or DB trigger on users.login = lower(login), and a one-time migration to merge any existing case-variant duplicate rows.

title: DELETE finalize bypasses period_summaries unique constraint when the row doesn't exist yet
description: DELETE /api/planning/finalize PATCHes period_summaries with `finalized_at=null, finalized_by=null` filtered by (period_id, user_id, segment_code). If no row matches the filter (e.g., wrong segmentCode, or a race where the upsert hasn't landed yet), Bitrix returns 204 with no rows updated and the handler returns success:true. The caller assumes the plan is now un-finalized but the underlying row may still have finalized_at set (under a different segment code casing) or never existed. There is no Prefer:return=representation to expose 'updated 0 rows'.
impact: Admin un-finalizes a plan; UI says success; on next reload the plan is still locked. No way to distinguish 'truly unfinalized' from 'silently no-op'. In particular if the admin types a slightly different segmentCode (case/whitespace), they get a false success and the lock stays.
recommendation: Add `Prefer: return=representation` and parse the response body. If the array length is 0, return `{success: true, updated: 0, warning: 'no row matched'}` so the UI can warn 'nothing was unfinalized — check filters'. Bonus: also force the segmentCode to a canonical form before filter.

title: init-snapshot user UPSERT uses ignoreDuplicates=true but profile metadata becomes immutable
description: init-snapshot upserts the users row with `ignoreDuplicates: true` (resolution=ignore-duplicates). This means once a user row exists with a given id, init-snapshot will NEVER update full_name/role/region. If a manager switches region or is promoted to RM/director after their first snapshot, subsequent init-snapshot calls will not refresh the profile — the user row stays stale until a planning POST runs (which uses merge-duplicates).
impact: Region-based dashboards may render with stale region_code or stale role for managers whose first interaction with the system was via init-snapshot but who have not yet saved their planning. Multi-region RMs added after onboarding can be invisible until a manual repair.
recommendation: Either always rely on /api/planning POST to maintain user metadata (and only INSERT in init-snapshot when missing), or switch init-snapshot to merge-duplicates with explicit COALESCE-style fields. Document the contract on which endpoint 'owns' the users row.

title: sync-dlq retry/skip silently succeeds with 0 rows when state already changed (no audit signal)
description: POST /api/admin/sync-dlq retry/skip PATCHes meeting_syncs with filter status=failed. If two admins click 'Retry' on the same row simultaneously, the second PATCH matches 0 rows (status is now 'pending') and the wrapper returns `{error: null, data: []}`. The handler returns `{ok: true}` and logs `[sync-dlq] retry triggered by ... for sync ...` — falsely implying a retry was just queued. Same for skip: a row already manually-skipped by another admin will log a second 'manually skipped by ...' line that didn't actually do anything.
impact: Operator audit log is misleading: it appears that two retries happened, but only one rows-affected actually flipped. Forensic investigation of why a sync ran (or didn't) trusts these log lines. Low severity because behavior is still safe, but breaks 'who did what' attribution.
recommendation: Inspect data.length (the wrapper already requests return=representation via update()). If 0 rows updated, return `{ok: true, noop: true}` and skip the console.log, OR log a distinct 'no-op (already X)' line. UI can then show 'nothing to do — already pending'.

title: clients-page.tsx — 2797 LOC with 40+ inlined sub-components in one file
description: Single .tsx module hosts the top-level `ClientsPage` plus 40+ internal helpers and sub-components: `PageTitle`, `ClientsMonthFilter`, `HeroVykonannya`, `HeroBaza`, `HeroActivation`, `HeroContacts`, `FilterPill`, `CategorySection`, `ReservedSection`, `ClientRow`, `NumCol`, `PctCol`, `ClientExpand`, `ClientInfoBlock`, `ClientFocusBlock`, `ThreeMonthHistory`, `EventsTimeline`, `TabBtn`, `EventCompactRow`, `SeminarRow`, `PlanFactByBrand`, `PlanFactHeader`, `PlanFactBrandRow`, `BirthdayBanner`, plus ~15 pure helpers (`canonicalSegmentCode`, `toUICategory`, `toUkrainianChip`, `initials`, `cleanBrandName`, `parseMonthLabelToYM`, `isHiddenProperty`, `formatMonthLabel`, `currentYearMonth`, `fmtYMShort`, `lastNMonthsBefore`, `buildHeaderSubtitle`, `pluralUaClient`, `pluralUaYears`, plus constant maps `BRAND_NAMES`, `BRAND_CODE_ALIASES`, `MONTH_PREFIXES_LOWER`, `HIDDEN_PROP_PATTERNS`, `UA_MONTHS`, `UA_MONTHS_SHORT`). The file mixes container logic, presentational rows, expanded-detail layouts, timeline rendering, and brand pivot logic.
impact: Code review surface is huge — a one-line change to e.g. `ClientRow` requires loading 2800 lines of context. Hot reload is slow, test isolation is impossible (none of these sub-components are exportable for unit tests). Merge conflicts on this file are now common as parallel features (meetings, claims, focuses, activation) all add inline pieces here. Onboarding new devs on this module is measurably painful.
recommendation: Split by responsibility into a `clients/` directory: `hero-cards/{HeroVykonannya,HeroBaza,HeroActivation,HeroContacts}.tsx`, `client-row/{ClientRow,ClientExpand,NumCol,PctCol}.tsx`, `client-row/sections/{ClientInfoBlock,ClientFocusBlock,ThreeMonthHistory,EventsTimeline,PlanFactByBrand}.tsx`, `helpers/{brand-codes,categories,month-labels,plural-ua}.ts`. Target: `clients-page.tsx` becomes <500 LOC container only. Constants `BRAND_CODE_ALIASES`, `HIDDEN_PROP_PATTERNS`, `MONTH_PREFIXES_LOWER`, `UA_MONTHS*` lift into the helpers files.

title: Duplicated date-parse pattern `split('-').map(Number)` repeated in 11+ files
description: The identical ISO-date-to-local-`Date` pattern appears in at least 21 spots across 11 files: `three-month-rule.ts:38,79`, `mityng-types.ts:96,107`, `clients-page.tsx:278,911,1543,2285,2290`, `planning-form.tsx:491,493`, `period-filter.tsx:16`, `rm-dashboard.tsx:205,210`, `manager-dashboard.tsx:95,144`, `director-dashboard.tsx:116,121`, `company-overview-dashboard.tsx:102,269`, `meetings/mock-data.ts:346`, `use-my-clients.ts:213`. Pattern is `const [y, m, d] = str.split('-').map(Number); new Date(y, m - 1, d)`. Additionally clients-page.tsx:1548 parses DD.MM.YYYY format inline with no shared helper, and CALLING `new Date(weekEnd)` directly is an `architecture-check.mjs` anti-pattern (lines 122-125) — meaning the codebase already knows this is fragile but has no positive shared API.
impact: Every dev that touches month/period logic must re-derive the right shape (sometimes only year+month, sometimes y/m/d, sometimes weekEnd). Bug fixes (e.g. UTC-shift, leap-year) must be applied N times. The `architecture-check.mjs` rule prevents the WRONG pattern but doesn't enforce the RIGHT one. New code paths can still parse dates wrongly and only break in QA.
recommendation: Add `src/lib/dates.ts` exporting `parseISODateLocal(iso)`, `parseYearMonth(ym)`, `parseUkrainianDate(ddMmYyyy)`, `formatYearMonth(date)`. Replace the 21 inline call-sites mechanically (codemod). Extend `architecture-check.mjs` to ban raw `new Date(<string>)` everywhere except `dates.ts` itself.

title: Hardcoded business-critical logins in source (`sdu@`, `itd@`, `rm.odessa@`) with no env override
description: Three identity-coupled constants are hardcoded in source and consumed by 11 server routes + components: `ADMIN_LOGINS = ['itd@emet.in.ua']` (line 44, grants admin role override in `adaptLogin`), `DIRECTOR_PROXY_LOGIN = 'sdu@emet.in.ua'` (line 55, used by `/api/admin/company-overview` and Action 5 proxy in `/api/onec/route.ts:164`), `MULTI_REGION_RM_OVERRIDES = { 'rm.odessa@emet.in.ua': ['ODS','NLV'] }` (line 67, grants company-wide read access in `/api/planning/aggregate`, `/api/planning/finalize`, `/api/planning/route.ts`, `/api/onec/region-stats`). The file comment itself flags lines 78-84 as «костиль» (workaround) and notes «Якщо Sasha звільниться — оновити тут». Any role change (new director, Pashkovska transfers) requires a code commit + Vercel deploy by a developer with repo access, not by an admin via UI.
impact: Any HR change (Director rotation, RM transfer, new admin) requires a developer cycle (commit, review, push, deploy ~3min). High operational risk on personnel changes — a Director leaving means a Saturday-night patch. Also a secondary security concern: these constants effectively act as access-control tokens but live in plain source — an accidental rename or merge mistake silently revokes/grants company-wide data access.
recommendation: Move to env vars (`ADMIN_LOGINS=`, `DIRECTOR_PROXY_LOGIN=`, `MULTI_REGION_RM_OVERRIDES=JSON`) read once at server boot with a single defaulting function. Better: store in a Supabase `system_overrides` table with a `/admin/access-overrides` admin-only UI. Add an audit log when overrides are read so role-elevation is traceable.

title: Dead feature flag `FEATURES.PLANNING_DISABLED` still wired through 11 places, blocked by stated «cleanup commit»
description: `FEATURES.PLANNING_DISABLED` is `false` (line 34) and the comment on lines 27-32 explicitly says: «2026-05-13: вимкнено, контроль перейшов до window-lock... Видаляємо повністю окремим cleanup commit-ом після того як подовгу пересвідчимось що window-lock стабільно працює у проді.» Today is 2026-06-11 — almost 4 weeks past the disable date — yet the kill-switch still bleeds across 11 spots: `isPlanningWritesAllowed()` helper, `MaintenanceBanner` component (line 18), `/api/planning/route.ts:130`, `/api/planning/finalize/route.ts:54`, `/api/planning/confirm-activities/route.ts:73`, `planning-form.tsx:106` (`isMaintenanceLocked`). The MaintenanceBanner is rendered in every dashboard and `planning-form` even though it can never display.
impact: Every developer reading planning/finalize/confirm-activities routes wastes time understanding a code-path that cannot fire. New maintenance scenarios (e.g. partial regional lock) get implemented on top of a dead abstraction instead of replacing it. Mental model debt accumulates: «is this banner the active one or the legacy one?»
recommendation: Delete `PLANNING_DISABLED` from `FEATURES`, simplify `isPlanningWritesAllowed` to `(login) => true` and inline at call sites, remove `MaintenanceBanner` component + all imports, drop the 503 branches in the three API routes, remove `isMaintenanceLocked` from planning-form. One commit, ~50 LOC deleted net. If true emergency lock is ever needed again, window-lock UI at `/admin/planning-locks` already covers it.

title: `mock-data.ts` exports demo plans/clients/forecasts gated only by `NEXT_PUBLIC_DEMO_LOGIN` env — risk of accidental enable in prod
description: `mock-data.ts` (209 LOC) still ships demo fixtures: `MOCK_USERS` (4 fake `@emet.com` accounts including a fake Director with `managedUsers: ['rm.dnipro@emet.com']`), `getDemoTMSummaries`, `getDemoClientStats`, `getDemoClientsForPlanningResponse`, `getDemoForecastsPETARAN`, `getDemoGapClosuresPETARAN`, plus a hardcoded `DEMO_CLIENTS` array with real-looking PIBs (Бліндовська, Гімішлі, Воронько, Андрущук, Астровська). The login route gates demo only via `process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_DEMO_LOGIN !== 'true'` (auth/login/route.ts:60), with no domain check. If anyone ever flips `NEXT_PUBLIC_DEMO_LOGIN=true` in Vercel prod env (intended for preview) and pushes to prod, the fake `director@emet.com` immediately gains a session as Director. The flag is `NEXT_PUBLIC_*`, so it's also visible in the client bundle — anyone can probe whether demo is on.
impact: Single env-var misconfiguration grants full Director role bypassing 1C entirely. The PIBs in `DEMO_CLIENTS` look like real client names and could leak as «sample data» in screenshots or analytics. The whole module is also still importable by production code (clients-page.tsx imports `SEGMENTS` from it), so it's bundled and not tree-shaken away.
recommendation: (1) Move all `getDemo*` functions and `MOCK_USERS` into `src/lib/__demo__/mock-data.ts` only imported under `if (process.env.NODE_ENV !== 'production')` guards; (2) extract `SEGMENTS` (real brand metadata) into `src/lib/segments.ts` — it's not mock data; (3) in the login route, also assert `process.env.VERCEL_ENV !== 'production'` so even a flipped flag can't grant demo sessions in prod; (4) replace the realistic-looking demo PIBs with «Demo Client A/B/C» to remove leak risk.

title: `architecture-check.mjs` covers structural shape only — no LOC/complexity/duplication gates
description: Current `architecture-check.mjs` (178 LOC) enforces three things: (1) required files exist, (2) required exports exist (string-match `export function X`), (3) required usages exist (`director-dashboard.tsx` must contain `RegionAccordion` etc), (4) two specific anti-patterns: `new Date(currentPeriod.weekEnd)` and `c.category === 'active'` in planning-form. Gaps observed: NO LOC ceiling (clients-page.tsx is 2797 lines unchecked), NO duplicate-pattern detection (the 21-site date-parse duplication is invisible), NO `any` count (38 occurrences across 22 files unmonitored), NO hardcoded-login scan (the costyli would not flag a 4th override quietly added), NO dead-code/unused-export check, NO check that `isPlanningWritesAllowed` callers shrink to zero after kill-switch is removed.
impact: The script catches structural regressions (someone deleting BrandRow) but lets quality drift happen invisibly. Each new feature can inline another 300 lines into clients-page.tsx, copy-paste another `split('-').map(Number)`, or sneak `: any` — none caught by CI. Reviewers do the catching, but only when they notice.
recommendation: Extend with: (a) LOC ceiling per file (warn >1200, fail >2500) with explicit allowlist for migration files; (b) ban `new Date(<string>)` outside `src/lib/dates.ts`; (c) `: any` budget per directory (current count + 0 new ones); (d) regex-scan of feature-flags.ts to ensure email overrides only via env (or whitelist constant size); (e) post-cleanup gate: after the `PLANNING_DISABLED` removal, fail if the symbol reappears.
